Banasiak

Mathematics Educator · Retired Engineer · Author

An ex-engineer who found her real work in the classroom. I teach mathematics as a way of thinking, building lessons that begin with curiosity and end with students solving problems on their own.

y = kx Constant of proportionality
1.4
A lesson from my classroom: change k, and the line through the origin changes with it. The relationship stays proportional.
01 / Philosophy

Why I teach

Teaching is not only an act of imparting knowledge. It is the thing that makes me feel most alive.

Teaching is an art of giving back. It is passing down the knowledge I have learned, so it can serve as an example, an instrument, and an inspiration to my students, whom I call our future. Allen Banasiak · personal definition of teaching

I earned a degree in engineering and built my first career around the start of iPods, tablets, and iPhones. A supervisor with twenty years of experience said one line that stayed with me: "Allen, you can be a teacher." Years later that idea became a reality.

I keep returning to an old line from Confucius: "Choose a job you love, and you will never have to work a day in your life." For me that job is teaching. The little kid who shaded a reading log to track every finished book, who drew the pictures from library stories onto paper, is the same person who now learns alongside her students.

Before becoming a teacher I wrote two children's books. They are where my interests live, and they became the backbone of why I teach.

Children's book

Oh Deer! Yes Dear

A mother's unconditional love for her son, told through animals and nature as metaphor.

Children's book

Cheer Cheer Cheer

The American Goldfinch ventures place to place, meeting birds whose similarities and differences shape a small society.

02 / How students learn

Attention is the gateway

Inattention is one of the top reasons students cannot learn. Getting and holding attention is where learning starts.

Cognitive neuroscience points to four environmental factors that arouse and hold attention. I plan for all four when I co-teach a math group, especially with students who arrive labelled as high-needs.

Drawn from Dale Schunk, Learning Theories: An Educational Perspective.

a

Importance

Scaffolding and guided questions placed inside the student's zone of proximal development.

b

Novelty

Letting a group explore and discover first, before any problem solving begins.

c

Intensity

Voice and body language as emphasis. A quiet prompt to rethink, a clear "you got it" when it lands.

d

Movement

Hand-picked markers and a standing Post-it board, so participation is visible and physical.

An active mind needs an active body

Our ability to focus on hard cognitive tasks is tied to physical activity. Children grow restless and distracted with prolonged sitting. When each math group stands and works at the board, small movements stay available, restlessness drops, and attention climbs.

Standing workstations turn fidgeting into participation rather than disruption.

Foundations

The theories I build on

My practice rests on constructivism. From Piaget's cognitive constructivism, I let students work first as "lone scientists," reasoning on their own. From Vygotsky's social constructivism and the zone of proximal development, I then move them into pairs and groups to build knowledge through talk.

Behavioural theory shapes how I arrange and reinforce a lesson. Cognitive theory reminds me that what students do with information, how they attend, rehearse, and store it, decides what they actually learn.

03 / Effective teaching

The one skill I want students to keep

Problem solving. It is the through-line of almost every math objective, and it carries into decisions, real life, and careers long after my class.

Education is not the learning of facts but the training of the mind to think. Albert Einstein

Effective teaching comes from matching the right learning theory and strategy to the content, then proving it in a lesson plan where the objectives and the activities line up. To move students toward mastery of problem solving, I rely on three strategies that build on one another.

01
Activate

First Activity

A hands-on group task that wakes the senses and sparks curiosity. Students measure a string around a circular board to feel circumference before any formula appears. One student who usually put their head down led the group through it. The exploration becomes working memory the rest of the lesson can build on.

02
Connect

Modeling with prior knowledge

New information connects back to what the First Activity established, now treated as prior knowledge. I model the method, scaffold during the zone of proximal development, and watch for who needs a verbal prompt, who can run on their own, and where misconceptions hide.

03
Check

Independent Practice

The exit ticket, and a cornerstone of formative assessment. It shows where students struggle, tells me what to reteach, and gives each student a quick sense of what they understood by the end of class. Concise, and always aligned to the day's objective.

04 / Relationships

The four Cs of action

Knowing a student comes before teaching one. As Ted Sizer put it, "I cannot teach a child I do not know."

C-1

Communication

The first move belongs to the teacher and the school: open the conversation with families and students, and keep it going.

C-2

Connection

The goal of communication. A real partnership with parents and students, and a sense of belonging that boosts focus and commitment.

C-3

Compassion

When students and families know the teacher cares, they listen more and the connection lasts. Compassion lets fear take a back seat.

C-4

Consistency

Steady routines and clear expectations turn the first three Cs into habit. Structure is where students learn what to expect, and what is expected of them.

05 / Professional growth

How my practice changed

Student teaching moved me from applying theory on my own to building lessons and classrooms with other people.

What shifted

  • From solo to co-teaching.Planning and teaching beside a mentor teacher and a special education teacher, sharing the room and the decisions.
  • Parallel over station teaching.After running five stations, we analysed the data and settled on parallel teaching, grouping by mathematical readiness. Blue Engine PD sharpened the choice.
  • Data as the most powerful tool.Team review of our assessment software let us chunk results by question and reteach exactly what students missed.
  • Materials kept simple.My mentor modelled that the simpler the material, the easier it is to understand. The percent methods became arrows from underlined words to the equation.

Sample materials

Growth goal

Master co-teaching

Keep building the craft of teaching beside a general or special education teacher, through district training and one-on-one planning with my mentor.

06 / Classroom evidence

Artifacts from the room

Word walls, worksheets, differentiated tasks, and the small tools that make a concept visible. Tap any artifact to enlarge.
